articles.title: Why Were Prehistoric Insects So Huge? New Study Flips Old Theory
articles.summary: Three hundred million years ago, dragonflies with 27-inch wingspans ruled the skies. Scientists long believed that high oxygen levels allowed these giant insects to grow so large. But a new study in Nature challenges that idea. Researchers found that insect flight muscles contain very few oxygen-delivering tracheoles—even in giant species. This suggests oxygen wasn't the limiting factor. The true reason for their enormous size remains a puzzle, opening up new questions about evolution and prehistoric life.

articles.title: Venus Reveals a Massive Lava Tube—Bigger Than Any on Earth
articles.summary: Scientists have discovered the first lava tube on Venus, and it's enormous—possibly a kilometer wide. The finding came from reanalyzing radar data from NASA's Magellan probe, which mapped Venus in the 1990s. A collapsed "skylight" near the volcano Nyx Mons revealed the tube. This discovery is important because lava tubes on the Moon could serve as shelters for astronauts. But Venus's extreme surface conditions—crushing pressure and scorching heat—make it unlikely humans will ever explore this tube in person.

articles.title: Scientists Create 'Optical Tornadoes' That Could Revolutionize Quantum Tech
articles.summary: Researchers have generated swirling beams of light called "optical tornadoes" inside a liquid crystal, achieving a stable, low-energy state for the first time. The light twists like a vortex and can be controlled with an electric voltage. This breakthrough could lead to simpler, more scalable photonic devices for optical communication and quantum technologies. The team used self-organizing materials instead of complex nanotechnology, opening new possibilities for miniature light sources.
